text: Welcome everyone to today's lecture about text recognition. My name is Andreas Fischer. I'm a professor in computer science at the University of Applied Sciences and Arts Western Switzerland in Freiburg and I'm also a lecturer at the University of Freiburg. A few words about myself. I did my PhD thesis in 2012 at the University of Bern on the topic of handwriting recognition in historical documents. The fields of research that were involved were essentially pattern recognition, automatic recognition of text, using methods for artificial intelligence and especially machine learning. In the past years have been involved in many projects that addressed transcription, keyword spotting, transcription alignment, layout analysis and ground truth creation, topics all of which I hope to explain a bit more in detail in this lecture. On the right I made a collage of a couple of documents I've been working on, I'm still working on regarding text recognition. You can see medieval old German manuscripts, there is also papyrus at the bottom, you can see engravings in stone, these are old Vietnamese steels and all at the bottom there is a letter correspondence from Heinrich Bollinger that has to be transcribed automatically and converted into a digital edition. So today's lecture has the following topics. First I will talk about why we want to do automatic text recognition and what are the different tasks we have to solve. Then I will attempt to give an insight into the inner workings of a text recognition system without going into algorithmic details but to convey a certain feeling how the machine proceeds to recognize characters and words. And finally I will give a few pointers to literature but also software that might be interesting in this domain. So why automatic text recognition? The overarching goal is to preserve our written cultural heritage in a way that we can access old manuscripts not only in libraries such as this very nice Baroque room of the Abbey Library of St. Cole but conveniently in an electronic form. Having access to a document is not only having access to the images but ideally also to the contents especially for text recognition having access to the actual text. This would then allow us to browse in the library similar as we are used to browsing on the internet. We can search by keywords and find pages, documents of interest according to some search text. So the search will not only be an author search or by year etc. as we are used to from metadata in the libraries but actually go into the books and navigate through the individual words. In order to achieve this automatic reading there are a couple of steps that are usually solved in sequence. The very first task is to have a look at the scanned page and to find zones that actually contain the text. They can be organized in columns, paragraphs, family lines and we might even be interested to separate individual words. For a human it's a rather simple task even if we don't know the language we can apprehend somehow the structure of writing and except in very difficult cases I think even a child could tell us where are the different lines on this scanned page. It turns out however for the machine this is one of the hardest tasks. The machine does not necessarily know about the textual structure in general and handwriting in particular can be in very creative force on a page so this is already a very first difficult challenge to solve. Once we have identified zones of text and typically the goal is to have the text at the line level. This is currently the unit of processing that is preferred for the text recognition systems. This image of the cutout line will then be given to a text recognizer and the text recognizer goes from left to right through the image reads and outputs a sequence of characters and words accordingly. Once we have this transcription we can also think about the third step which is the text retrieval. This is the internet search I have been talking about but with this special aspect that the transcription that we have produced automatically usually contains errors. So what we need here in this third step is to include kind of a fuzzy search that the system helps us find words that are maybe written a bit differently or contain OCR errors. Among these three tasks let us focus on the second one which is the main topic of this lecture text recognition. In this scenario the classical goal is as I mentioned before the transcription input is a text line image and output is a sequence of machine readable characters. Machine readable is important in that context because afterwards we want to do text search for example on these transcriptions. However this is one of the hardest tasks that has to be solved. Recognizer does need a robust model for the individual character shapes that can be quite different even within the same manuscript but essentially the problem is that there are so many different historical manuscripts, languages and scripts out there that building character models, optical character models for all of them is a tremendous task. Also the language should be known because during the recognition some statistics about the language what kind of word is more likely to follow given a certain number of recognized words. This kind of information also helps to produce a nice transcription but more often than not the resources needed to train such a transcription system are simply not available. Another task that is a bit less involved I would say is keyword spotting here. We are no longer interested to produce a complete transcription but we have a few select words that we are interested in and we go through our text lines that we have already cut out and we try to return those text lines where we believe the keyword to be present. This can be done also by template matching for example the user selects one example of the word we are interested in and then the machine can go through the lines and do some image to image matching to see where the word might be present or not. But even this task part of experience requires a couple of resources essentially text lines images for which we know the correct transcription to train some character models. If this is not available there is a third task that is even a bit easier for the machine which is the task of transcription alignment. In this scenario we have the image and we already know the text. This might be a plausible scenario for example for the Bible where we know the text of the Bible and we have a new image material that needs to be aligned with it or coming back to the papyrus if there is the Iliad we know what is written but we need maybe to align fragments of papyrus with the text. What the machine does here is to find the best possible correspondence between individual words of the transcription and the image taking into account that there might be some parts of the image that have no correspondence to the text and also some parts of the text that have no correspondence to the image. It's not only to find the start and end position of the words it's also a bit more involved if the transcription does not match 100% what is visible on the image. As I've mentioned from a machine learning point of view to do these tasks the transcription alignment can be done with probably the least resources. That's the easiest task and the full transcription is the hardest task and this should only be attempted if there is a lot of material ready for the machine to learn from. We will talk about that later. I've been talking a lot about handwriting but more generally what is possible today in terms of text recognition. When it comes to modern print I would say it can be considered as mostly solved. There are commercial applications available. There is Abbey Fine Reader for example that can deal pretty well with scanned modern prints. The fonts are known to the system. If the scan quality is high enough I would say then almost perfect optical character recognition can be attempted. This will give us at least access to the characters on such a page but interpreting for example the logical layout. Where are the articles? Where are the titles? Where are the paragraphs? Where does an image belong to? To which article? These are still quite open questions so this is why I would say it's not solved but it's maybe mostly solved at least when it comes to the actual work of recognizing characters. When it comes to historical print it's already much more difficult. The condition of the paper for example can lead to artifacts on the scans. The fonts are not well known to modern OCR systems. The layouts might be unfamiliar with respect to modern prints but there are some studies especially a larger European project called Impact that have shown that in this area since its print models can be trained fairly accurately with reasonable resources. So I would say here historical print is partially solved. If you want to create an electronic edition of a historical print you will still need to invest a lot of effort to reduce the number of errors. When it comes to handwriting however the challenges are enormous. Handwriting is very individual. I think it's clear for all of us when you compare your own handwriting with that of your friends. The character shapes that need to be learned are much more variable than a printed font. Also the layout on these pages is more challenging because with handwriting we are rather free even if there are of course examples where monks follow a very clear structure and also try to copy a character according almost font like according to a predefined style. However overall I would say this domain of handwriting recognition is more unsolved than solved. There are character error rates that go easily to 10% word error rates that go easily to 20%. So from five recognized words one will be wrong and these kind of errors usually prohibit the publication of clean electronic editions. There always needs to be some human involved if you want to have an error free result. However for tasks like indexing, finding specific keywords even having an 80% correct word recognition is of course enormously valuable when you compare it with a raw scan where you only have access to the image itself. There is also another problem for handwriting recognition that was already stated by an American philosopher Seire in 1973. Speaking about the problem of handwriting recognition it was clear to him pretty soon that in order to recognize the handwriting you need to segment it into characters first so that you can then recognize the characters. However to segment the characters you actually need to understand to recognize the character in turn. So recognition requires segmentation, segmentation requires recognition. This is not necessarily the case for printed text where the fonts are so regular that you can easily separate individual characters and then recognize them. If you look here at the example I'm pretty sure especially for distinguishing the start and end of the N and the T of this word even for humans it's quite difficult to get a perfect segmentation. This problem occurs whenever characters are touching. It's especially difficult for cursive handwriting with a lot of different ways how the characters are linked. So what modern recognizers do to solve the problem they actually solve the two problems at the same time. They recognize, they segment, they recognize, they segment and they find the best overall solution. But this is one of the main reasons why handwriting is, well maybe not the main reason, but one of the reasons why handwriting is so much harder than printed text recognition. Now that we know what we want to solve let me try to give you an impression how text recognition is solved, how the machine approaches the problem. And for that we first need to consider how a machine actually perceives an image. You can see on the top left an image of some wooden balls lying on a table and if you zoom in into this rectangle that is marked in white what the machine actually receives are numbers. It's a matrix of numbers, one number per pixel in this grayscale image ranging from 0 to 255. And you can also consider this as a kind of a landscape on the top right. You have a representation of the numbers that goes higher the higher the number is. So it's kind of a landscape of little hills that we need to analyze. This is now the surface of a wooden structure on the table, but if you look at handwriting it will exhibit similar shapes to be analyzed. So how can we treat this matrix of numbers? Let us consider the example of character recognition in the context of voting. Let's from a German example so we would vote J for Ja or Yes or we would write N for 9 or No. In this scenario let us assume that the ballots are scanned somewhere and the region where the characters are written are then extracted so that we end up with these images that contain either the handwritten character J or N. We also apply some pre-processing to the image. Instead of having numbers between 0 and 255 we decide either it's a white pixel or a black pixel depending on the characters or the black pixels or the character foreground and the white pixels are the character background. If we have a look at all the possible images that we can find for our machine let's assume those rectangles with the character in it they have a dimension of 25 times 20 pixels. So we have 500 pixels and each of the pixels is either white or black. So the first pixel is either white or black this gives us two options then if we take the second pixel as well we have two times two already four different configurations so white white white black white and black black and so on and so forth. So combinatorially speaking we have two to the power of 500 different images that we can produce that might be the input of our system 10 to the power of 150. This is of course impossible to list and to label correctly each of them. Instead what the machine does is to reduce the problem by extracting so-called features. These are numerical characteristics of the text that will then allow us to simplify the recognition. For example we know that Anne is quite symmetrical as a character and Jay is not so we could count we could cut the image in half so let's say we draw a line here in the middle of the image and then we count how many black pixels are on the left hand side and how many black pixels are on the right hand side and it's clear for the Jay there are much more pixels to the right than to the left so if we divide left by right we will get a number that is clearly lower than one and for the Anne it's more or less symmetric so we might expect a number on average for this feature that is around one and by looking at the problem from this perspective we can then say that the J's are the images where this ratio X is rather small and the Anne's are the images for which the ratio is close to one at least larger than for the J's and quite naturally we can then put the threshold on this value X and say whatever is below let's say 0.75 is considered a J and everything that's above this threshold is considered an Anne and with that we have already built our first character recognition system. To look at the problem with a bit more detail we can do a so-called probabilistic classification which is also known as the patient classifier. In order to do that we first need what is called a training set so these are images of J's and Anne's that have been labeled by a human imagine maybe a thousand images and person sits in front of them and for each of them decides this is a J this is an Anne this is an Anne this is a Jay and so on and then in this training set we know for each image what it is. Afterwards we count how many times we see J and how many times we see an Anne and we count a bit more in detail we create so this is still our feature X and we create bins for example this bin could be between 0 and 0.1 how many times did we find an image where the value of the feature has been between 0 and 0.1 then between 0.1 and 0.2 and so on. By doing that and normalizing the value we will get this kind of density distribution at the distribution of our feature and we can see that most of the J's have a value of about the third and most of our Anne's have indeed a value on average of about one but this distribution takes really all the details into account and then we can choose a suitable threshold that is optimal in the sense that if we now classify all the J's below the threshold all the images below this threshold as J's we can immediately see that this area here this will be the errors we expect to make because these Anne's will be wrongly classified as J's. On the other side all these Anne's are correctly classified and all these J's will be wrongly classified as Anne's so this gives us this overall Bayesian error under this curve and you can try to move the threshold to the left and to the right and you will see that the error that you are expected to make for the classification will only increase. So here the threshold is put at the optimal position. With some assumptions actually so the assumption here would be that there is an equal number of J's and Anne's in general so we don't have to wait the two distributions. But with this in mind we can try to make a classification that is even a bit more reliable. We can say that well we don't want that much errors so we reduce the errors by defining two thresholds. We can say everything that's below the first threshold is a J and the error that we expect is smaller and everything that is above the second threshold is an Anne and the error that we expect is also smaller than before. This depending on how we put the thresholds we can actually reduce the expected error to some threshold that's acceptable for the problem. So you might think what's acceptable for voting 1% error, 2% error. This can be defined and thanks to the training set we can then put the thresholds in a way that the estimated error is within a reasonable limit. And what happens in between? So these are the images where our recognizer does not know what to do and here we can simply reject automatic classification and give back the image to a human to decide in these more difficult cases. Of course the more samples we reject the more work manual work for the human but by using this procedure here we are sure that the human only needs to look at difficult cases and not images that are rather clear and can be recognized automatically. So far we have looked at one feature. This was the fraction of black pixels to the left divided by the number of black pixels to the right. And so this is again our feature that we have looked at so far and we have seen on average it's a third for the chase and about one for the ants because they're symmetric. We can now also add more features and following the same logic we can say how many black pixels are in the top part of our image divided by the bottom part. And again for the J because there is a lot of black pixels on the bottom. The second feature here will also have a value of let's say around the third for the J's and around one for the ants. And we are now in a two dimensional plane and we can see here the circles they represent images that are J's and the squares they represent images that are ants. And the goal of classification is now to separate the two for example with a straight line to say on the left side here of the straight line these will be the J's on the right side there will be the ants and the learning process if you want is to find the good positioning of this straight line. This is for example what support vector machines do or also the perceptron algorithm optimizes the positioning of such a separating plane. As you can see or assume here from the two dimensional case it should become easier to classify the two classes because we have more information. Now what was not so clear here we have maybe an example. There is a J that has a relatively large value for the first feature but because we now also look at the second feature we can actually find out that it is nevertheless a J and not an M. And with that we can add more features we can think about characteristics ourselves that are pertinent for the character and like that we can construct larger number of features and the better separation of our recognition of our characters. What I've explained so far is a rather classical perspective on machine learning on how to create a text recognition system. As you are certainly aware since maybe 10 years I would say a novel methodology called deep learning has changed the way machine learning is done nowadays. What is the difference? So in the previous example I said we start with raw data then imaged and we apply some pre-processing what we did is we distinguished the handwriting foreground. We said this is a black pixel and the background here we said that's a white pixel which gave us some binarized cleaner data. Afterwards we decided ourselves what kind of features we would like to have to have a look at to decide essentially what character it is and we've defined two. The fraction of black pixels left divided by right and top divided by bottom and this then gave us some abstract representation of our character. And finally we trained a model to distinguish the chase from the ends and in order to do that we needed a training set. And in this training set we had a lot of images in the example it was 1000 images for which a human assigned a label J or N to each one of them. And thanks to this training set we could then find a good model that can decide between chase and ends. What happens in deep learning is that this pre-processing and feature extraction part is learned as well. So the machine looks at the training data and just by analyzing it it finds out what are good ways to pre-process it, what are structures that are interesting and then also what are the features essentially that should be taken into account to decide which character it is. Like that in the deep learning area you only have to provide the raw data and the training set and the rest is done by the deep learning system. The advantages are at the moment it's clear that the deep learning approaches lead to typically better results, significantly better results than classical machine learning approaches but the disadvantages are that you need more training data because the machine needs to learn more from the data. And also it's not always or mostly not intuitive what kind of features that the machine extracts automatically. So if you want to explain at the end why the J is a J and why the N is an N it gets a bit complicated and this can lead to troubles. If you think about the voting example it might be problematic that you cannot explain why this vote was considered as a yes or a no. So there are some pitfalls with deep learning but overall it's a fascinating technology that tries to analyze and understand the important parts of the data to distinguish the different classes by itself. The implementation of deep learning is typically done with artificial networks that have a large number of layers and each layer in that context is considered as a further abstraction. So the first layers would deal with some basic structures in the image like vertical or horizontal changes and intensity and then the middle layers would be building a bit more abstract features as we did with counting the top and the bottom number of black pixels and the final parts of the model would then be the ones that distinguish based on the automatically extracted features and the different classes. So I've talked a lot about the training set and especially in the deep learning area there is nothing more valuable than a good training set of high quality and also of a large quantity. So this is the key to make historical manuscripts or historical prints digitally available to extract the text. This is to provide the machine with a lot of learning samples. Those learning samples are also called ground truth. The truth about what is written on the image is a bit ambiguous because of course even humans will not always agree on what is written and there might be variants and so on. But from a machine learning perspective this will be the targets that are given to the machine to learn. So the machine in the end will be guided towards recognizing this character as a D and this character as an E if we give it a good ground truth. What is ground truth? For text recognition it actually has not only the transcription part so we need of course to say on this image this is the exact sequence of characters but there is a second part which is sometimes also quite difficult to provide is where are the text lines on the scanned page image. If you remember from the beginning of the lecture there is a processing part where you try to understand the layout of a page, the columns, the paragraphs and the text lines so this also needs to be learned. And for that ideally around each text line you would put this kind of polygon to be exact and also separated from the lines above and below and then you can basically tell the system this is a text line. And if you provide the system a lot of examples like that it will begin to do the layout analysis part better. And then within each of these polygons you provide also the transcription and then the system can begin to learn character shapes, it can also begin to learn the intrinsic statistics of a language, what is the characters that typically follow each other, what are the words that follow each other, what are the different variants maybe of the same word. All of that can be implicitly learned if you provide the system with enough learning material. One point that is important when we talk about the transcription here for the ground truth for text recognition, ideally this transcription is accurate at the character level in the sense that it corresponds one to one with what is visible. For example the sceptre here can be written out in a transcription because it makes more sense for a human when reading the text. However, for machine learning aspects it would be better to annotate it here with a special character for the abbreviation to say here on the image I have this special abbreviation character so this part of the transcription is what ideally is available. If a transcription has written out abbreviations like here it's admissible, it's still possible to learn but it will provide targets to learning the optical model that are a bit noisy. So the machine learning system will react a bit less well to your training material if you have words like these, they should be as much as possible accurate at the character level to what is visible on the image. Alright, with that I hope I could give you a good impression on what text recognition is about from a computer science perspective. I would like to finish the lecture with a few pointers. First in terms of literature, if you are interested in pattern recognition in general there is the pattern classification Bible by Dutthard and Stork. It has especially a very nice introduction that explains a bit similar to what I've tried to do with the classification of Js and Anns, what are the basic mechanisms behind classification. I can really recommend that as an introductionary work that is also quite comprehensive when it comes to classical machine learning methods. If you are interested especially in the deep learning models there is a standard book these days from Goodfellow, Benjo and Gubil. The book is called Deep Learning from 2016 and Benjo is one of the Turing Award winners on the topic of deep learning together with Hinton and Jan Le Brum. These are people at the very core of the development of the methods and it is written in a very didactic way so I can recommend that as a starting point. And finally if you want to be more closely where the newest research happens every year I can recommend the conference series, the International Conference on Document Analysis and Recognition. It was a b-annual conference but it is now every year and there from a computer sense point of view the newest technologies and text recognition are presented and exchanged. It's quite a large conference but it typically also has some workshops dedicated to specific topics, satellite events and I can mention especially the historical document imaging and processing workshop called HIP that is still strongly focused on computer science but welcomes also participants from the humanities through to exchange and often datasets are discussed on historical manuscripts and then there is also a workshop, a new workshop computational calligraphy that was introduced and this workshop especially warmly welcomes people from the humanities to in an attempt to bring a bit together the scientists from computer science and humanities. And finally I cannot do a lecture about computer science without citing some code. There is a lot of software available these days that allow to do handwriting recognition, it was not the case when I started my PhD a few years back but now it is handwriting recognition or text recognition in general is actually quite accessible. The first three, four links are links to free software that a programmer can use. These are links to code, to source code and in the case of the transformer OCR the fourth link it's a implementation in hugging phase it's quite nice API simple to use and very powerful handwriting recognition system. It is not recommended for persons who were new to programming but if you have some experience especially in Python these are all options you can use off the shelf on your own to perform text recognition. And then there is also more and more commercial products that are available for historical documents I would like to highlight the Transcribals platform this came out of the European research project and is now in a self-sufficient mode let's say with a subscription fee available and it has a very interesting interface as you don't need to be a programmer to use it you can upload your documents and then apply handwriting recognition models to it and download the results and use them for your research. There is also a Google Vision component that does handwriting recognition with astonishing accuracy. This is also not for free essentially but one of the options you might consider to have a high quality handwriting recognition result. High quality and this is my final caveat still means let's say 5 to 10 percent of the characters are wrong and let's say 10 to 20 percent of the words are wrong. This is not something that is going to be solved within the next couple of years I fear but there are many applications and use cases that are interesting even if the text recognition does not have a perfect quality. With that I end for today I thank you for your attention and I'm happy to get in touch with you should you have any questions.

This will give us at least access to the characters on such a page but interpreting for example
the logical layout.
Where are the articles?
Where are the titles?
Where are the paragraphs?
Where does an image belong to?
To which article?
These are still quite open questions so this is why I would say it's not solved but it's
maybe mostly solved at least when it comes to the actual work of recognizing characters.
When it comes to historical print it's already much more difficult.
The condition of the paper for example can lead to artifacts on the scans.
The fonts are not well known to modern OCR systems.
The layouts might be unfamiliar with respect to modern prints but there are some studies
especially a larger European project called Impact that have shown that in this area since
its print models can be trained fairly accurately with reasonable resources.
So I would say here historical print is partially solved.
If you want to create an electronic edition of a historical print you will still need
to invest a lot of effort to reduce the number of errors.
When it comes to handwriting however the challenges are enormous.
Handwriting is very individual.
I think it's clear for all of us when you compare your own handwriting with that of
your friends.
The character shapes that need to be learned are much more variable than a printed font.
Also the layout on these pages is more challenging because with handwriting we are rather free
even if there are of course examples where monks follow a very clear structure and also

However overall I would say this domain of handwriting recognition is more unsolved than
solved.
There are character error rates that go easily to 10% word error rates that go easily to
So from five recognized words one will be wrong and these kind of errors usually prohibit
the publication of clean electronic editions.
There always needs to be some human involved if you want to have an error free result.
However for tasks like indexing, finding specific keywords even having an 80% correct word
recognition is of course enormously valuable when you compare it with a raw scan where
you only have access to the image itself.
There is also another problem for handwriting recognition that was already stated by an
American philosopher Seire in 1973.
Speaking about the problem of handwriting recognition it was clear to him pretty soon
that in order to recognize the handwriting you need to segment it into characters first
so that you can then recognize the characters.
However to segment the characters you actually need to understand to recognize the character
in turn.
So recognition requires segmentation, segmentation requires recognition.
This is not necessarily the case for printed text where the fonts are so regular that you
can easily separate individual characters and then recognize them.
If you look here at the example I'm pretty sure especially for distinguishing the start
and end of the N and the T of this word even for humans it's quite difficult to get a
perfect segmentation.
This problem occurs whenever characters are touching.
It's especially difficult for cursive handwriting with a lot of different ways how the characters
are linked.
So what modern recognizers do to solve the problem they actually solve the two problems
at the same time.
They recognize, they segment, they recognize, they segment and they find the best overall
solution.
But this is one of the main reasons why handwriting is, well maybe not the main reason, but one
of the reasons why handwriting is so much harder than printed text recognition.

Instead of having numbers between 0 and 255 we decide either it's a white pixel or a black

If we have a look at all the possible images that we can find for our machine let's assume

So the first pixel is either white or black this gives us two options then if we take
the second pixel as well we have two times two already four different configurations
so white white white black white and black black and so on and so forth.
So combinatorially speaking we have two to the power of 500 different images that we

This is of course impossible to list and to label correctly each of them.
Instead what the machine does is to reduce the problem by extracting so-called features.
These are numerical characteristics of the text that will then allow us to simplify the

For example we know that Anne is quite symmetrical as a character and Jay is not so we could count
we could cut the image in half so let's say we draw a line here in the middle of the image
and then we count how many black pixels are on the left hand side and how many black pixels
are on the right hand side and it's clear for the Jay there are much more pixels to
the right than to the left so if we divide left by right we will get a number that is
clearly lower than one and for the Anne it's more or less symmetric so we might expect
a number on average for this feature that is around one and by looking at the problem
from this perspective we can then say that the J's are the images where this ratio X
is rather small and the Anne's are the images for which the ratio is close to one at least
larger than for the J's and quite naturally we can then put the threshold on this value
X and say whatever is below let's say 0.75 is considered a J and everything that's above
this threshold is considered an Anne and with that we have already built our first character
recognition system.

which is also known as the patient classifier.

on.

On the other side all these Anne's are correctly classified and all these J's will be wrongly
classified as Anne's so this gives us this overall Bayesian error under this curve and
you can try to move the threshold to the left and to the right and you will see that the

So here the threshold is put at the optimal position.
